spec_match.rationale: Models the Open UI explainer's nested-group composition exactly: outer horizontal toolbar holding an inner submenu with the `extend` token. Toggles `extend`, `no-memory`, `wrap`, and outer axis — all four token combinations the spec defines.

interactive.rationale: Four real toggles drive the attribute string live on both outer and inner groups, a polyfill-style keydown handler honours the attribute so the page works in browsers without native support, and a key-events log surfaces what each press did.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb names extend / no-memory / wrap / axis specifically and promises live toggling — page delivers all four.

edge_cases.rationale: Token combinations are explored, but the comment 'Polyfill-style arrow handling so visitors without the native impl see behaviour' undersells a real risk: the polyfill might diverge from native behaviour, especially for `extend` (which the spec defines via shadow-tree-aware focus delegation). No edge case for: focus inside a popover, focus inside an iframe child, or a third level of nesting.

references.rationale: Links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5637601087193088 and the Open UI explainer in see-also.

design_system.rationale: Variables throughout, opt buttons use class-driven on/off state with the design system tokens, no static-card.

openQuestions.title: Mark the polyfill explicitly as illustrative
openQuestions.detail: The keydown JS is intended as a polyfill but visitors who don't read the source will assume the keymap matches native focusgroup. Add a small banner: 'this page polyfills focusgroup for visualisation — exact behaviour may differ from the Chrome 146 implementation' so the spec-conformance promise isn't oversold.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate a third level of nesting
openQuestions.detail: Real menubars often nest 3 levels (menubar → menu → submenu). Add a third inner group so the `extend` token's transitive composition is visible and the spec's recursive semantics are honoured.

openQuestions.title: Show `no-memory` versus default memory side-by-side
openQuestions.detail: The `no-memory` token resets to the first item when the group is re-entered. Currently the visitor has to toggle the flag and re-Tab to see the difference — easy to miss. Show two parallel groups (one with, one without) so the reset behaviour is immediately visible.

summary: Most ambitious focusgroup concept — explores the full token vocabulary with live toggles and a usable polyfill. Mark the polyfill nature explicitly, ship a 3-level nesting case, and stage `no-memory` next to its default counterpart for a clearer behavioural contrast.
